attributes.stem:
A 54-year-old woman is presented to the Emergency Department by a family member as they are extremely worried about her mental health. She has not left her room for four days and could not be coaxed by family members to eat any meals. Her medical history includes major depression, for which she takes sertraline. She has a history of a previous hospital admission following a suicide attempt two years ago. She has never experienced manic symptoms. She does not take any other regular medications.

She is admitted to the psychiatric ward. It is now day two of her admission, and she has been verbally unresponsive when spoken to by the nursing staff or doctors. She has been immobile in bed, and staff have been unable to get her to eat or drink since her admission. On examination, she appears unwell with vital signs within normal limits. Investigations have ruled out any organic cause of her symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the strongest indication that this patient is a candidate for electroconvulsive therapy?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe symptoms of major depression, including catatonic features and refusal to eat or drink. In such cases, electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) may be considered as an urgent intervention.

Refusal to eat or drink is the strongest indication for ECT in this scenario. This behaviour, along with her immobility and unresponsiveness, suggests a severe depressive episode with possible catatonic features. These symptoms can be life-threatening and require immediate intervention. Antidepressant medications can take weeks to become effective, making ECT a more appropriate choice for rapid symptom relief in such severe cases.

While a diagnosis of major depression can be an indication for ECT, it is usually considered after other treatments have failed or in cases of treatment-resistant depression. In this case, the severity of the patient's current symptoms is more relevant than the diagnosis alone.

A previous suicide attempt is a serious concern but not necessarily an immediate indication for ECT. However, if a patient has severe, persistent suicidal ideation that doesn't respond to other interventions, ECT might be considered. In this case, the stem doesn't mention ongoing suicidal intent, though a thorough risk assessment including assessment of suicide ideation is a crucial part of the work up of any case of major depression and would be needed in this case.

The patient's age of 54 years is not a specific indication for ECT. While ECT can be used across various age groups, age itself doesn't determine the need for this treatment.

The presence of psychosis can be an indication for ECT, particularly in cases of treatment-resistant psychotic depression. However, the stem doesn't provide any information suggesting that this patient is experiencing psychotic symptoms such as delusions or hallucinations.

In summary, the patient's refusal to eat or drink, combined with her catatonic-like symptoms, presents the strongest indication for ECT in this scenario. ECT can provide rapid relief in severe cases of depression, especially when there are life-threatening symptoms or catatonic features present.
learningPoints: Electroconvulsive therapy is primarily used in the treatment of depression that is resistant to other therapies.
Electroconvulsive therapy may be required in severe depression where the patient is refusing oral intake or has severe ongoing suicidal intent.
Catatonia is a condition where electroconvulsive therapy might be considered as a first-line treatment option.
references: Electroconvulsive therapy. In: Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd. [online]. 2019. Available at: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/index

Vanelle JM, Sauvaget-Oiry A, Juan F. Indications de l'électroconvulsivothérapie [Indications for electroconvulsive therapy]. Presse Med. 2008 May;37(5 Pt 2):889-93. French. Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0755498208000900
Baghai TC, Möller HJ. Electroconvulsive therapy and its different indications. Dialogues Clin Neurosci. 2008;10(1):105-17. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3181862/
